I would reflect the wonder in thine eyes,
And capture all within a waking dream.
    So let me dwell within thy memory's hold,
    A tale of love, more precious far than gold.


The fragile bloom that opens to the day,
And sheds its petals with the fleeting breeze,
Doth teach me how my mortal joys decay,
And leave me sighing 'neath the somber trees.
The summer's heat, that burns with fiery zest,
Doth fade and yield to autumn's chilling air,
And all that seemed secure and truly blest,
Doth vanish, leaving naught but dark despair.
But if thy love, a constant star, doth shine,
No season's change can dim its ardent flame,
And though my form to dust shall soon decline,
My spirit shall forever speak thy name.
    So let me hold thee close, ere life departs,
    And engrave thy image on my beating hearts.


The winter's frost, that binds the frozen stream,
And silences the music of the spring,
Doth mirror well my solitary dream,
And all the lonely suffering I bring.
The barren branches, reaching to the sky,
Doth show my soul, devoid of summer's grace,
And as the weary seasons hasten by,
I find no comfort, nor a resting place.
Yet, in my heart, a hidden warmth I keep,
A spark of hope, that will not be denied,
That when my frozen spirit wakes from sleep,
Thy loving glance shall be my guiding guide.
    So let me brave the cold, and wait for thee,
    My sun, my spring, my only joy to be.


The golden coin, that doth the merchant's hand
Enrich with wealth, and buy him earthly gain,
Is worthless, when I seek to understand,
The priceless treasure that thy love doth reign.
The jeweled crown, that doth adorn the king,
And grants him power, majesty, and might,
Can not the joyous melody e'er sing,
That fills my heart, when bathed in thy pure light.
If I were a monarch, on a distant throne,
And all the kingdoms of the world were mine,
I'd gladly yield them, for a love unknown,
The sweet affection that is so divine.
    So let me be thy subject, and thy slave,
    And in thy loving service, find my grave.


The restless waves, that beat upon the strand,
And never cease their murmuring and flow,
Doth mirror well my heart, by love unmanned,
And all the constant longing that doth grow.
The hungry wolf, that prowls the lonely wood,
And seeks his prey with hunger in his eyes,
Doth not compare to where my soul hath stood,
A captive bound, beneath thy sweet surprise.
If I were granted but a single wish,
To grant my heart its ultimate desire,
I'd ask to be within thy loving dish,
And nourished by thy ever-burning fire.
    So let me be thy feast, thy willing prey,
    And in thy boundless love, I'll gladly stay.


The sturdy ship, that bravely rides the storm,
And bears its cargo safe to distant lands,
Doth not protect my frail and beating form,
From tempests brewed by thy commanding hands.
The fortress wall, that stands with might and pride,
And guards the city from the foeman's blow,
Can not my secret longing truly hide,
Nor make my ardent admiration slow.
If I were a poet, with a lofty mind,
I'd weave a verse, immortal and sublime,
To tell the world what wonders I do find,
In thee, my love, beyond the reach of time.
    But though my verse be humble, and my plea,
    My heart's true homage shall belong to thee.


The faithful dog, that waits beside his lord,
And greets him home with wagging tail and leap,
Doth not possess the love that I have stored,
Within my breast, where precious memories sleep.
The blooming rose, that opens to the morn,
And sheds its fragrance on the passing air,
Doth not compare to thee, who art, since born,
The fairest flower, beyond all mortal compare.
If I were a craftsman, with a cunning art,
I'd fashion thee a throne of purest gold,
And place thee there, the monarch of my heart,
A tale of love, forever to be told.
    So let me serve thee, as a loyal knight,
    And find my solace in thy radiant light.


The winter's snow, that blankets all the land,
And stills the world with silent, soft embrace,
Doth not impart a peace to my command,
Nor bring a smile unto my weary face.
The frozen river, bound with icy chain,
Doth not reflect the turmoil in my breast,
Where passions rage, and hopes do wax and wane,
And grant my troubled spirit any rest.
Yet, in my soul, a single wish doth burn,
To feel the warmth of thy returning smile,
And from thy love, my frozen heart to turn,
And walk with thee, for many a happy mile.
    So let me dream of spring, and of thy grace,
    And find in thee, my season and my place.


The shifting sands, that drift upon the shore,
And change their form with every passing tide,
Doth not possess the constancy I swore,
When in thy loving gaze I did confide.
The fragile glass, that shatters at a touch,
And leaves behind a thousand jagged shards,
Doth not reflect the pain that I feel much,
When thy sweet glance my hopeful heart discards.
Yet, if my love, like diamond, firm and true,
Can never break, nor ever lose its gleam,
Then let me offer all I am to you,
And live forever in this waking dream.
    So let me be thy rock, thy steadfast ground,
    Wherein thy purest happiness is found.


The summer's heat, that doth the landscape bake,
And leave the earth in thirst, with cracking soil,
Doth not compare to fires that do wake,
Within my breast, and set my soul to toil.
The hungry lion, with his fearsome roar,
Doth not inspire the terror that I feel,
When I do think of what I do adore,
And fear thy absence, and thy stern repeal.
If I were given strength, beyond my might,
To face all perils, and to conquer fear,
My greatest battle would be for thy light,
To keep thee ever, ever, ever near.
    So let me be thy shield, thy loyal guard,
    And bear all wounds, though they be sharp and hard.


The fading embers, in the hearth's cold grate,
Doth speak of warmth, that now hath passed away,
And leaves behind the cold and lonely state,
That marks the ending of a brighter day.
The silent owl, that watches from the tree,
With knowing gaze, upon the sleeping world,
Doth not perceive the sorrow that holds me,
As by thy absence, my own soul is hurled.
Yet, if thy love, a never-dying flame,
Can banish shadows, and the night dispel,
Then let me whisper low thy precious name,
And in thy glowing heart forever dwell.
    So let me be thy flame, thy steady light,
    And banish from my soul all earthly night.


When Phoebus doth his golden chariot drive,
And banisheth the shadows of the night,
My waking soul begins itself to thrive,
And drinks the sun's most gladdening light.
But ere that beam on weary eyelids fall,
My dreams, like spectres, haunt my troubled breast,
And whisper fears that hold my heart in thrall,
Denying slumber, peace, and needed rest.
So is my day with ghostly visions marred,
As if a midnight shade did cling to me,
My waking joys by phantom pains are barred,
And freedom's gate is locked, I cannot flee.
   Yet hope shall dawn, though present darkness reign,
   And chase away this melancholy's pain.


The painted bloom that doth the spring adorn,
And scents the air with perfumes passing sweet,
Too soon shall see its tender petals torn,
And lie as dust beneath the hurried feet.
The summer's sun, that warms the fertile earth,
And bids the tender shoots to rise and grow,
Shall yield its strength and shrink from winter's birth,
And cast a pale and melancholy glow.
So youth and beauty, though they seem so bright,
Are but a flicker, ere they fade away,
And manhood's power, and wisdom's learned might,
Must bow at last to time's relentless sway.
   Then let us live and love whilst we have power,
   And seize the joy of every fleeting hour.


My love, like to a river, deep and wide,
Doth bear me onward with its constant flow,
No earthly shore can cause its course to slide,
Nor shall its destined destination know.
It doth not ebb with fickle fortune's tide,
Nor freeze when winter's chilling winds do blow,
But ever onward, resolute, doth stride,
And in its depths, a steadfast purpose show.
Though tempest-tossed, and rocks may block its way,
It finds new channels, ever onward pressed,
And shall not cease, until the final day,
When all is stillness, and it finds its rest.
   So may my heart, in love's embrace so sure,
   Forever beat, and through all trials endure.


The silent stars, that in the heavens gleam,
And watch the turning world with patient eye,
Do seem to mock my solitary dream,
And whisper tales of joys I can't descry.
For whilst they shine, a beacon in the black,
My own poor spirit finds no guiding ray,
But treads a lost and solitary track,
And wanders blindly through the darkened way.
I gaze upon their distant, cold abode,
And yearn for company, a kindred soul,
But find myself upon a lonely road,
Where no warm hand doth comfort or console.
   O cruel fate, that leaves me thus alone,
   To feel the pangs of sorrow, deeply sown.


When I behold thy countenance so fair,
A sudden flush doth paint my pallid cheek,
And all my thoughts are lost beyond repair,
And I, poor wretch, no eloquent words can speak.
Thy voice, a music that doth fill the air,
Doth make my spirit tremble, frail and weak,
And in thy gaze, I find a wondrous snare,
Wherein my captive heart doth meekly seek.
Thou art the sun that banishes my gloom,
The moon that guides my passage through the night,
The fragrant flower that doth my senses bloom,
And fills my world with all its purest light.
   So shall I worship thee, my goddess dear,
   And hold thee ever in my heart so near.


The fickle wind, that doth at pleasure blow,
And stirs the leaves upon the forest trees,
Can change its course, and swiftly onward go,
And leave behind no lingering unease.
So is the love of many, weak and low,
That shrinks and flees from every passing breeze,
And when adversity begins to grow,
They cast it off, and seek their selfish ease.
But mine, my love, is of a sturdier kind,
That fears no storm, nor shrinks from winter's chill,
A steadfast flame, that shall not be confined,
But burn with constant passion, strong and still.
   So let the doubters waver and depart,
   My truest love resides within my heart.


The fleeting hours, that steal our youth away,
And wrinkle brows where once smooth skin did lie,
Do mark the progress of our briefest day,
And bid us cease our labours, and to die.
The strength of manhood, and the bright display
Of beauty's bloom, do vanish by and by,
And leave us old and withered, in decay,
Beneath the gaze of an indifferent sky.
Then let us not our precious moments waste,
In idle dreams or foolish, vain regret,
But let our lives be with true purpose graced,
And sow the seeds of deeds we won't forget.
   For time, though swift, shall leave a lasting name,
   For those who live with honour and with fame.


Thy gentle hand, that doth my spirit mend,
And banisheth the shadows of despair,
Is like a balm, on which I can depend,
A precious treasure, beyond all compare.
Thy whispered words, that softly do transcend,
The noise and clamour of this worldly care,
Do teach my wounded soul to comprehend,
A peace that worldly goods can ne'er declare.
Thou art the haven, where my ship may rest,
When tempests rage and billows rise so high,
The guiding star, that leads me to the best,
And wipes the tear from my most weary eye.
   So let me ever in thy love abide,
   And feel thy presence ever at my side.


The golden coin, that doth the miser's hand
Enslave with greed, and hold him in its thrall,
Can buy no joy, nor happiness command,
But builds a lonely, self-created wall.
The jewels bright, that sparkle through the land,
And tempt the covetous, to risk their all,
Bring naught but sorrow, when they understand,
Their true worth is not in such things to call.
For wealth of spirit, and a heart sincere,
Outshine the brightest diamond's fiery gleam,
And love's true bond, that holds us ever dear,
Is richer far than any waking dream.
   Then let us seek what truly makes us blest,
   And find in virtue, everlasting rest.


The changing seasons, in their ordered round,
Do teach us lessons, of a profound might,
How death doth yield to life, on hallowed ground,
And darkness flees before the morning light.
The winter's snow, that doth the earth surround,
Gives way to spring, with verdant, fresh delight,
And barren fields, where naught could then be found,
Are clothed anew in nature's glorious sight.
So shall my soul, though plunged in deepest woe,
Find solace, and its former strength regain,
And like the seed, that deep within doth grow,
Break forth anew, and banish every pain.
   For e'en in loss, a deeper strength is found,
   When hope's fair blossoms rise from sorrow's ground.


When Fortune smiles, and all doth seem to go
As we have wished, and all our hopes succeed,
Then many friends do round our bounty flow,
And offer solace in our every need.
But when the clouds of trouble start to show,
And fortune's favour doth itself recede,
These fickle friends, like summer birds, do go,
And leave us to our solitary creed.
True friendship's worth, in trials it is known,
When darkest hours do press with heavy hand,
A loyal heart, on which we can be thrown,
A steadfast rock, on which we firm can stand.
   So let us cherish bonds that will not break,
   For true friends' love, our weary souls will make.


The silent tears, that from mine eyes do stream,
Are testament to sorrows I conceal,
A hidden pain, that haunts my waking dream,
And makes my wounded spirit sadly reel.
I wear a mask, to hide the inner gleam,
Of anguish deep, that time cannot unseal,
And play a part, as in a tragic theme,
While secret wounds my weary body feel.
But thou, my love, with understanding bright,
Dost see the truth, behind the outward show,
And with thy comfort, banish all the night,
And help my troubled, broken spirit grow.
   So in thy love, a healing balm I find,
   And leave the shadows of my past behind.


When Phoebus doth his golden chariot guide,
And sheds his light upon the waking earth,
My weary soul, which in the darkness sighed,
Doth find again a cause for joyful mirth.
The birds do sing their carols in the trees,
A melody that doth my spirit lift,
And gentle whispers borne upon the breeze,
A welcome, sweet, and unlooked-for gift.
The flowers bloom with colours bright and bold,
And chase away the shadows of the night,
A fairer prospect than was ever told,
That fills my heavy heart with pure delight.
So let me greet the dawning of the day,
And cast my former sorrows far away.


The fleeting hours, that steal our youth away,
And carve their furrows deep upon the brow,
Do mock our efforts to prolong our stay,
And make us slaves to time's relentless vow.
The bloom of beauty, like a summer's rose,
Doth fade and wither ere the autumn's chill,
And strength, which in our vigorous youth it shows,
Doth wane and leave us feeble and quite still.
Yet in the mirror of thy comely face,
I see a beauty that doth time defy,
A lasting charm, a never-ending grace,
That shall not with the passing seasons die.
So let us cherish what we hold so dear,
And banish from our hearts all mortal fear.


My ink doth flow, but finds no worthy theme,
To sing the praises of thy peerless worth,
My muse doth slumber, lost in idle dream,
And gives no tribute to thy beauty's birth.
My verse doth falter, weak and lacking might,
To capture half the splendour of thy face,
My tongue is tied, though I desire to write,
The wondrous virtues of thy gentle grace.
Yet still I strive, with labour and with pain,
To weave a rhyme that may thy favour find,
And though my efforts prove to be in vain,
My constant love shall ever be enshrined.
So take these words, though they be poor and few,
And know my heart beats only, love, for you.


When slumber's hand doth close my weary eyes,
And dreams do conjure visions fair and bright,
My soul takes flight to where my true love lies,
And basks within her ever-shining light.
I walk with her through fields of purest gold,
And hear her laughter, like the sweetest chime,
A story in my dreaming to be told,
That doth transcend the bounds of earthly time.
But when the dawn doth break and I awake,
And find myself alone in waking hours,
My heavy heart begins again to ache,
And longs again for sleep's enchanted bowers.
So let me dream, though waking bringeth pain,
And in thy presence, find my bliss again.


The fickle crowds, that throng the city's street,
Do ebb and flow like tides upon the shore,
Their fickle favour, transient and fleet,
Is sought by many, yet desired by few for more.
They cheer the victor, then with scorn they frown,
Upon the loser, fallen from his height,
And build up heroes, only to cast down,
Their fleeting idols, in the fading light.
But thou, dear friend, whose steadfast heart I know,
Dost stand apart from this capricious throng,
Thy constancy doth ever brightly glow,
And makes my spirit feel profoundly strong.
So let the multitude their favour lend,
Thou art the truest, most beloved friend.


The deepest night, though cloaked in darkest hue,
Doth hold within its breast a secret gleam,
Of distant stars, whose light doth pierce anew,
And paint the heavens with a silver dream.
The darkest clouds, that shroud the sun from sight,
Do promise rain to quench the thirsty land,
And in their gloom, a hope for coming light,
A brighter future, close at Nature's hand.
So though my heart be plunged in sorrow's shade,
And all my prospects seem to fade and die,
Thy love, a star that never can be swayed,
Doth shine upon me from the highest sky.
So let the darkness gather all around,
In thy dear light, my solace can be found.


When illness grips my frame and strength doth flee,
And fever's fire consumes my fading might,
I turn my thoughts to thy dear face, and see
A solace shining, banishing the night.
The weary hours, that crawl with leaden pace,
Are softened by the memory of thy smile,
And though I suffer in this lonely place,
Thy whispered promise doth my pain beguile.
So though my body languish and grow weak,
And all the world seems clad in somber hue,
Thy steadfast love, the solace that I seek,
Doth grant me courage to endure it through.
For in thy presence, strength doth reappear,
And banish all my doubt and every fear.


The seasons change, the winter yields to spring,
And summer's heat doth give way to the fall,
Each turning year doth new delights to bring,
And Nature answers to the primal call.
The leaves that bud, then bloom in emerald bright,
Do fade and fall, then rise again in green,
A constant cycle, bathed in changing light,
A wondrous, never-ending, vibrant scene.
But thy dear love, a season of its own,
Doth know no winter, nor a fading leaf,
Its verdant beauty ever brightly shown,
A joy that brings my spirit sweet relief.
So let the world its seasons ebb and flow,
Thy love's a summer that shall ever grow.


The fleeting shadows of this mortal life,
Do mock our plans and scatter our desires,
And leave us wounded by relentless strife,
And quenched by sorrow's cold and bitter fires.
The dreams we build, so fragile and so grand,
Do crumble swift before the winds of fate,
And leave us standing in a barren land,
Where naught remains but sorrow and its weight.
But thy true heart, a fortress strong and deep,
Doth offer refuge from the world's harsh storm,
And in its shelter, quiet I can keep,
And find a solace, safe and ever warm.
So let the world its changing fortunes cast,
Thy constant love shall evermore outlast.


When envious tongues do whisper tales of woe,
And spread their poison with malicious art,
And seek to strike where wounds do deeply grow,
And plant their seeds of discord in the heart.
When slander's breath doth chill the very soul,
And malice seeks to mar a spotless name,
And strive to drag us down into its hole,
And feed itself upon another's shame.
Then do I think upon thy honest mind,
Whose virtues shine with such a radiant glow,
That no false rumour can my spirit bind,
Nor make my loyal sentiments to go.
So let them speak their venom, sharp and keen,
Thy truth protects me, fairest, noblest queen.


The sweetest song, that doth the ear delight,
And makes the weary spirit lift and soar,
Is but a fleeting sound, and then takes flight,
And leaves the silence knocking at the door.
The richest feast, that doth the palate bless,
Doth vanish quick, and leaves a hunger keen,
And all the joys that earthly lives possess,
Are but a moment, then they are unseen.
But thy dear love, a never-ending tune,
Doth echo ever in my grateful breast,
A constant balm beneath the sun and moon,
That grants my longing spirit perfect rest.
So let the world its passing pleasures boast,
Thy love's the treasure that I cherish most.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
She treads upon the ground.
   And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
   As any she belied with false compare.


The wasting of our strength in fruitless quest,
Is lust's pursuit, and ere it finds its aim,
Is mad, and blind, and wholly dispossessed,
Enrag'd, and drown'd in an eternal shame.
All this the common eye doth plainly see,
Yet none can shun the lure that doth entice;
The end of all our striving is to be,
Lost in the toils of passion's subtle vice.
For all that seems most worthy to be sought,
Is but the fleeting pleasure we embrace,
And in its chase, our reason comes to naught,
And we are ruin'd in that sinful space.
   So are we lost, by this desire we hold,
   And all our better selves remain untold.


My love is like a fever, burning bright,
That craves the cure which feeds the very ill;
It doth consume me with a fierce delight,
And mocks my reason with a wicked will.
My sober sense, that would my course amend,
Doth leave me, and I, desperate, now embrace
The sweet damnation I cannot transcend,
The pleasing poison that doth seal my race.
My flesh doth hold me captive to its sway,
And love doth bind me with a silken chain,
And by this means my passion holds its day,
And reason doth my pleas forbear in vain.
   Yet in this fever I do languish still,
   And for this fever I do crave my fill.


Let not my absence dim thy loving thought,
Nor absence make thee find another's grace;
True constancy is dearly to be bought,
And time itself can not its strength displace.
Love is no fleeting passion of the eye,
That shifts and changes with the passing hour;
It is a star that watches from on high,
Whose steadfast beam doth hold eternal power.
No outward show can make true love depart,
Though beauty fade and youth begin to wane;
It dwells within the chambers of the heart,
And through all storms, its constancy doth reign.
   So let thy love be fixed, and ever true,
   As mine shall be, my dearest, unto you.


My mistress' eyes are not the sun's bright gleam,
Nor coral redder than her lips can show;
If snow be white, her skin, it doth but seem,
Black wires of hair upon her head do grow.
I have seen roses of a lovely hue,
But none so bright as in her cheeks I find;
And in her breath, a fragrance ever new,
A sweeter scent than perfumes of mankind.
I love to hear her speak, though common sound,
Her voice doth hold a music all its own;
I grant she treads upon the earthly ground,
Yet in her step, a queenly grace is shown.
   And though she boast no godhead's faultless art,
   She hath ensnared my true and loving heart.


When twenty summers have left their mark,
And sown deep furrows in thy brow so fair,
Thy youthful bloom, a momentary spark,
Shall be a memory, held beyond compare.
Then if you ask where all thy beauty lies,
Where all thy laughter and thy joyful days,
To say, within thy own forgetful eyes,
Would be a sorrow past all human praise.
How much more doth beauty, now in prime,
Lie treasured in a glass, and not in gold,
Whose brief existence yields to passing time,
And tales of youth are seldom to be told;
   But if such fate on beauty must befall,
   Then let me love thee, giving thee my all.


The time will come when thou shalt cease to sigh,
And find no solace in the lengthening day;
Thy weary limbs shall rest beneath the sky,
And all thy earthly cares be put away.
The songs of birds, the flowers of the field,
The summer's warmth, the winter's chilling blast,
Shall fade from memory, their power concealed,
When life's brief lease is finally recast.
No more shall love thy heart with passion fill,
Nor beauty hold thee captive to its sway;
Thy earthly race is run, thy soul is still,
And all thy striving hath passed away.
   Then shall I mourn thee, with a heavy heart,
   And all my dreams with thee shall now depart.


My love is like a sickness, deep and strong,
That feeds upon itself and grows more keen;
It doth preserve the ill, and doth prolong,
The endless yearning that remains unseen.
My reason, that the cure would fain impart,
Doth find its counsel wilfully ignored;
It leaves me to the passions of the heart,
And by desire, my better sense is gored.
The flesh doth hold me in its firm embrace,
And love doth bind me with a subtle thread;
And by this means my passion finds its place,
And reason's voice remains forever dead.
   Yet in this sickness I do find my bliss,
   And for this fever, I would gladly kiss.


When winter's breath, with chilling hand so cold,
Doth strip the branches bare and leave them stark,
And nature's vibrant colours turn to old,
As darkness settles, leaving its bleak mark,
So doth my spirit, when thou art afar,
Feel empty, barren, and devoid of cheer,
As if beneath a dim and fading star,
My life's true purpose doth begin to disappear.
But spring shall come, with blossoms fresh and new,
And life shall stir where slumber held its sway,
And all the world be painted in a hue
Of verdant hope, to greet the brighter day.
   So shall thy love, with warmth it doth impart,
   Revive the winter of my frozen heart.


The fleeting hours, like sand that slips away,
Through open fingers, never to return,
Doth steal the moments of each precious day,
And lessons that we never seem to learn.
So do I waste my youth in idle dreams,
And let the opportunities take flight,
Unheeding of the swift, departing streams,
That bear away my unillumined light.
But if thy love, a tutor wise and true,
Should guide my steps and point a better way,
Then might my life begin its task anew,
And chase the shadows of my past astray.
   For in thy counsel, wisdom I can find,
   To mend the errors of my erring mind.


The gentle stream, that murmurs through the dale,
Doth find its course, though stones may lie in way,
And ever onward, doth its journey trail,
Until it joins the ocean, far away.
So do I strive, with purpose in my breast,
To reach the haven where my heart finds rest,
To overcome each trial, put to test,
And give my all, with courage I am blessed.
But oft discouragement doth hold me bound,
And whisper doubts that sap my vital strength,
As if no solace ever can be found,
Nor journey's end, whatever be its length.
   Yet if thy hand, doth offer me support,
   I shall endure, and stand within my fort.


The tapestry that art hath wrought with skill,
Doth show forth scenes of grandeur and of might,
Where heroes strive, and conquer with good will,
And beauty shines with ever-constant light.
But when I seek to capture thee in rhyme,
My humble quill doth falter and doth fail,
To paint the portrait worthy of thy prime,
And tell a story that shall e'er prevail.
For human skill, however finely spun,
Can ne'er achieve what nature hath designed,
Thy grace and beauty, none hath e'er outrun,
A perfect marvel, of a wondrous kind.
   So shall my praise, though lacking in its art,
   Still speak the truth that dwells within my heart.


The silent owl, that through the darkness flies,
With watchful gaze and feathers soft and deep,
 doth spy its prey with keen and piercing eyes,
While all the weary, slumbering world doth sleep.
So do I watch thee, in my lonely hours,
And learn thy ways, with ardour in my soul,
As if to plunder nature's fairest flowers,
And make thy perfect beauty my sole goal.
But though I strive with all my might and main,
To capture essence of thy matchless grace,
My efforts prove but labour spent in vain,
Thou art a marvel that no words can trace.
   Yet still I gaze, with wonder and with awe,
   Subjected to thy captivating law.


When jealous envy doth with venom's sting,
Assail the heart and whisper words of spite,
And dark suspicion on its wicked wing,
Doth dim the day and banish all delight,
So do I feel, when rumours fly abroad,
And speak of falsehoods that do wound my name,
As if by falsehoods I am overthrown and awed,
And cast into a pit of burning shame.
But if thy trust, a shield of purest gold,
Doth guard my honour from each poisoned dart,
Then shall the truth, more precious than untold,
Prevail and mend my bruised and wounded heart.
   For in thy faith, my strength I do regain,
   And banish all the sland'rous, cruel pain.


When weary travelers, with thirst oppressed,
Do seek a spring, to slake their burning need,
And find sweet water, bringing them to rest,
A welcome solace, planting a hopeful seed,
So do I seek thy love, a fountain pure,
To quench the fever of my deep desire,
A balm that can my aching spirit cure,
And fan the embers of my soul's true fire.
But should this fount of kindness be denied,
Or turned to bitter waters, cold and strange,
Then shall my heart, with sorrow sorely tried,
Be left to wander, in a barren range.
   So grant me, love, thy ever-flowing grace,
   And find in me a loving, resting place.


When I do view the fading summer's light,
And feel the chill that heralds autumn's reign,
My soul doth shrink and tremble at the sight,
As all my former joys begin to wane.
The golden hues that kissed the verdant field,
Now turn to brown and dust, a sorry show,
And Nature's bounty, that was once revealed,
Doth yield to frost and winds that fiercely blow.
So too my love, though once it burned so bright,
Now flickers low, and seems to lose its flame,
And I am left to face the coming night,
With naught but sorrow whispered by my name.
   Yet even so, I pray this love endure,
   Though seasons change, my heart shall be most sure.


The tyrant Time, with ever-hungry maw,
Doth gnaw upon the fairest of the fair,
And makes of beauty but a fleeting law,
A phantom shape that vanishes in air.
The rose that blooms with velvet, blushing cheek,
Shall soon be withered, pale, and cast aside,
And laughter's echo, once so loud and sleek,
Shall fade and die, where sorrow doth preside.
So must I fear that all that I hold dear,
Shall fall victim to this ceaseless decay,
And love itself, that conquers every fear,
Shall yield at last unto the ending day.
   Yet if thy love in constancy doth stay,
   Time's cruelest hand shall steal it not away.


When I behold the stars that dot the night,
And ponder on their courses, vast and deep,
I wonder if there shines a greater light,
Wherein the secrets of the heavens sleep.
Do kindred souls in distant spheres reside,
Who feel the pangs of absence, sharp and keen,
And for their absent loves do softly chide
The cruelest fate that keeps them from their queen?
Or art thou, lady, but a single star,
Whose beauty outshines all the host above,
And drew me hence, from where my true joys are,
To worship at the shrine of thy sweet love?
   If so, then let me gaze and ever burn,
   Until the day my loving suit doth earn.


My muse, alas, doth languish and grow weak,
Her former fire, now but a dying ember,
And when I strive her potent aid to seek,
No worthy thought her vacant mind doth remember.
The quill lies idle, and the ink is dry,
My heart is heavy, and my spirit low,
And all the verses that I fain would cry,
Are lost within the stream of endless woe.
Perhaps my passion doth begin to fade,
Or love's sweet sting hath lost its former power,
And in this barren land, by Fortune made,
I cannot pluck a single, blooming flower.
   Yet still I hope, with lingering despair,
   To find again the verses that I swear.


The world doth rush, with hurried, restless pace,
And men do chase their fortunes, far and wide,
Each seeking for a better, nobler place,
Wherein their vain ambitions may preside.
But I, content with this my humble state,
And blessed with thy companionship so sweet,
Do find my joy, and laugh at cruelest fate,
As hand in hand, our happy spirits meet.
Let others toil and strive for empty fame,
And gain the world, yet lose their precious souls,
My only treasure is thy whispered name,
And thy dear love, that evermore consoles.
   For in thy eyes, a universe I find,
   A haven for my ever-wandering mind.


I swore to thee, my love, my life, my all,
That ne'er would absence dim my steadfast flame,
And though the winds of fortune fiercely call,
My heart would e'er repeat thy cherished name.
But cruel is absence, and the miles between,
Do cast a shadow o'er my brightest day,
And in the silence, what was once unseen,
Doth whisper doubts and lead my thoughts astray.
I see thy face in every passing cloud,
I hear thy voice in every rustling breeze,
And still I yearn, though I am forced to shroud
My lonely grief, and seek for sweet unease.
   Yet trust I thee, as I trust sun and moon,
   That we shall meet again, and that full soon.


The golden sun, that climbs the eastern sky,
Doth shed its warmth upon the sleeping earth,
And bids the flowers with bright colours vie,
And nature celebrates a new rebirth.
So too thy presence, when it doth appear,
Doth banish all the shadows from my breast,
And makes my weary, burdened soul feel clear,
And puts my troubled, anxious heart to rest.
Thy smile, a sunrise, chasing night away,
Thy voice, a music, sweet and soft and low,
Doth make me wish that I could ever stay,
And in thy radiant company bestow
   My grateful heart, to thee forever bound,
   Wherein my truest happiness is found.


When Fortune smiles, and all our hopes are met,
And joy doth overflow our happy cup,
We think this bliss will never know regret,
And raise our thankful voices, lifting up
Our souls to heaven, in a grateful prayer,
For all the blessings that we freely gain,
And cast aside all sorrow and all care,
Believing happiness shall still remain.
But ah, how fickle is this earthly state,
How swift the turn of Fortune's wheel doth go,
And plunges us from joy to darkest fate,
And leaves us weeping in the bitter woe.
   So let us hold our present pleasure fast,
   For earthly joys are never made to last.


My heart doth ache with unrequited love,
A bitter draught, that I am forced to drink,
And though I gaze at thee, as doth a dove
At cruelest hawk, my spirit starts to sink.
I offer up my soul, my fondest vow,
My truest faith, my every passing sigh,
But thou art cold, and dost not heed me now,
And turnest from my love with heedless eye.
Perhaps I am too bold, too plain, too rude,
And lack the grace that doth thy favour win,
Or else my passion is but misconstrued,
And thou dost think me but a foolish kin.
   Yet still I hope, though hope be thin and frail,
   That someday soon thy stony heart will quail.


When I do read the lines that poets write,
Of ancient loves, and battles fought and won,
I marvel at the power of their might,
To stir the soul, till day is done.
They speak of heroes, bold and strong and true,
Of ladies fair, with eyes that shone like stars,
And tales of passion, ever fresh and new,
That break down all of sorrow's bitter bars.
And though I cannot boast of deeds so grand,
Nor claim a fame that echoes through the age,
I still do hold my love within my hand,
And write it here, upon this humble page.
   For in this verse, my heart's true truth is shown,
   A love for thee, that stands to thee alone.


The sun descends, and paints the western sky
With hues of crimson, gold, and burning red,
A glorious sight, that doth command the eye,
As day doth yield, and darkness starts to spread.
So too, my love, thy beauty doth unfold,
And fills my soul with wonder and delight,
A story richer far than tales of old,
A radiant vision, shining ever bright.
Though seasons change, and empires rise and fall,
And mortal things are subject to decay,
Thy loveliness shall conquer over all,
And chase the darkest shadows far away.
   So let me gaze, and in thy beauty find,
   A joy eternal, for my loving mind.


The fleeting hours, like nimble steeds, do fly,
And bear away the bloom of youth so fair,
While seasons change beneath the changing sky,
And leave behind a visage etched with care.
The rosy cheek, where once true beauty dwelt,
Now shows the lines that time hath carved so deep,
And ardent fires, where passion brightly felt,
Do dwindle low, and in their embers sleep.
Yet in the soul, a light may still reside,
A wisdom gained from trials understood,
A calm repose, where gentler thoughts do guide,
And find in age a greater, truer good.
    So let us greet the years that onward haste,
    With memories of love, that cannot waste.


My lady's eyes, like stars that grace the night,
Do cast a beam that pierces through my soul,
And fill my breast with wonder and delight,
And make my heart obey their sweet control.
Her voice, a melody of sweetest sound,
Doth charm the air and banish all my woe,
And in her smile, a paradise is found,
Where all my joys and fondest wishes grow.
Her gentle touch, a balm to all my pain,
Doth soothe my fears and bid my doubts depart,
And in her presence, I am born again,
A captive made, with all my willing heart.
    So let me live within her loving gaze,
    And spend with her my bright and happy days.


The world's applause, a fickle, gusty breeze,
Doth puff men up and then doth cast them down,
And fortune's wheel, that spins with such unease,
Doth make a king, and then a beggar crown.
The praise of crowds, a shallow, empty thing,
Doth fade away when trials do draw nigh,
And flatterers, with honeyed words do sing,
But flee like shadows when the storm is high.
I seek no fame, nor wealth beyond my need,
But in a friend, a truth that shall endure,
A loyal heart, that plants a constant seed,
And offers solace, steadfast, firm, and pure.
    For in such bonds, true riches I do find,
    A lasting treasure, to my heart and mind.


When envious tongues do whisper slanders low,
And malice aims its venom at my name,
My spirit sinks, my inner strength doth go,
And I am lost in sorrow's burning flame.
The poisoned dart, though unseen, doth strike deep,
And leaves a wound that time cannot erase,
While watchful fears their anxious vigil keep,
And steal the smile from off my pallid face.
Yet if a truth within my breast doth lie,
And innocence my constant shield shall be,
Then shall these calumnies like shadows fly,
And leave my honour whole, unblemished, free.
    For what is said by spiteful, lying tongue,
    Is but a passing echo, briefly sung.


The gentle rain, that nourishes the earth,
Doth bring forth life where barrenness did reign,
And bids the seeds to rise, and prove their worth,
And banish sorrow, and relieve all pain.
So may thy love, a sweet and vital shower,
Descend upon my soul, so parched and dry,
And wake within me a forgotten power,
And chase the clouds from off my weary sky.
Let kindness fall, like dew upon the land,
And banish doubt, and conquer all my dread,
And take my trembling, supplicating hand,
And lead me forth, where brighter days are spread.
    For in thy grace, my spirit finds its zest,
    And in thy favour, I shall be most blest.


The changing moon, that waxes and that wanes,
Doth mirror well the fortunes of mankind,
Now bright with hope, now shadowed by our pains,
And leaving traces of what's left behind.
The fleeting tide, that ebbs and then doth flow,
Doth speak of change, that all things must embrace,
And in its rise, and in its gentle low,
We see the passing of time and space.
So let us learn, from nature's constant art,
To bear the storms, and hail the coming sun,
And in each change, to play a nobler part,
Until our earthly course is fairly run.
    For life itself, a river, onward flows,
    And finds its end, where sweet oblivion grows.


The silent stars, that watch from realms on high,
Do speak of peace, and secrets they do keep,
And in their gaze, beneath the midnight sky,
Our earthly woes do seem to fall asleep.
The gentle moon, a beacon in the dark,
Doth cast its beam, a comfort to the soul,
And leaves upon the world its silver mark,
And makes our weary spirits feel quite whole.
So let me gaze, and find a sweet repose,
In nature's calm, and beauty so profound,
And feel the peace that from the heavens flows,
And on this hallowed, silent ground.
    For in this quiet, where no sounds are heard,
    My heart finds solace, and my soul is stirred.


When absence doth thy presence steal from me,
And miles do stretch between our yearning hearts,
I find no solace, and no joy can see,
As sorrow plays its ever-bitter parts.
The world is dim, and colours lose their hue,
And music sounds a melancholy strain,
And every hour doth seem to bid adieu,
To joys I knew, and shall not know again.
Yet still I hope, with faith that will not bend,
That soon the distance shall no more divide,
And in thy arms, my journey finds its end,
And by thy side, my spirit shall abide.
    So let me wait, with longing deep and true,
    Until the joyful hour I meet with you.


Thy tender gaze, that meets my own with grace,
Doth fill my heart with comfort and with ease,
And in its depths, I find a resting place,
And banish all my troubles and my unease.
Thy gentle touch, a balm to all my pain,
Doth soothe my soul and bid my fears depart,
And in thy smile, my spirit lives again,
And finds its joy, and plays its happy part.
So let me dwell, within thy loving light,
And hold this moment, precious and so dear,
And banish shadows from my inner sight,
And keep thy memory ever ever near.
    For in thy love, my truest strength I find,
    A steadfast beacon, for my soul and mind.


When youthful ardour doth my senses seize,
And passion's fire doth burn with eager haste,
I oft do court the gentle, passing breeze,
And dream of pleasures that are held in taste.
But reason whispers with a sober sound,
That fleeting joys are but a transient shade,
And when the bloom of summer is unbound,
The frost of age shall leave the spirit frayed.
So let me temper this impetuous soul,
And seek a love that time cannot decay,
A steady flame to make my being whole,
And banish winter with its brighter ray.
    For beauty fades, though youth may brightly gleam,
    But constant truth shall ever be my dream.


The fleeting moments of this mortal span,
Like shadows dance and vanish in the light,
And all our dreams, since first the race began,
Are swept away upon the wings of night.
The mighty king, who rules with iron sway,
Must yield at last to death's impartial hand,
The wisest sage, who charts the starry way,
Shall find his knowledge cannot understand
The final summons, when the bell doth toll,
And earthly glories fade to dust and grey,
No wealth nor power can reclaim the soul,
Nor bribe the grim collector of the day.
    Thus, let us live, ere life itself doth cease,
    And find in virtue our eternal peace.


When winter's breath doth chill the verdant earth,
And frosty skies do banish summer's grace,
My thoughts do turn to seasons of my birth,
And seek the sun in some forgotten place.
The barren trees, that stand in naked pride,
Do mock the hues that once adorned their boughs,
And in their starkness, I do clearly spied
The passing years that furrow up our brows.
Yet hope doth whisper of the coming spring,
When life anew shall stir in dormant seed,
And joyful birds their cheerful anthems sing,
Dispelling gloom and satisfying need.
    So shall my spirit, though now cold and bare,
    Revive again, and banish all despair.


Thy beauteous face, a marvel to behold,
Doth shine with light, as doth the morning star,
More precious far than silver or than gold,
Whose radiant charms no earthly blight can mar.
Thy gentle eyes, like pools of deepest blue,
Do hold a spell that captures every gaze,
And in their depths, my truest thoughts do view,
And find themselves within their loving maze.
The rose's blush, the lily's snowy white,
Do pale and fade when set beside thy cheek,
And all the world doth seem a lesser sight,
When in thy presence, my poor words do speak.
    So let me live, and worship at thy shrine,
    And deem my life most wonderfully divine.


When fickle fortune turns her wheel around,
And smiles no more upon my humble state,
When bitter winds do howl with mournful sound,
And press me down beneath the heavy weight
Of worldly cares, that press upon my soul,
And dim the light that once did brightly burn,
I seek a refuge, to make my spirit whole,
Where true affection doth forever turn
To ease the burden, and to soothe the sting,
A steady hand to guide me through the storm,
A faithful breast, where comfort I may bring,
And find a shelter, safe and ever warm.
    For in such love, a treasure I do find,
    That far outweighs all riches of mankind.


The passing time, that steals our youthful bloom,
Doth leave its trace upon the fairest face,
And ushers in a melancholy gloom,
As years advance with unrelenting pace.
The laughter bright, the spirit free and bold,
Are tempered down by wisdom's solemn art,
And stories of the past are to be told,
With memories etched within the aging heart.
Yet in that wisdom, solace may be found,
A deeper knowing, and a clearer sight,
And on this ground, a truer joy is crowned,
That burns with steady, everlasting light.
    So let us age, with grace and quiet mind,
    And leave the fleeting follies far behind.


The summer's sun, that warms the fertile land,
Doth call forth life from slumber, deep and long,
And nature's bounty, spread on every hand,
Doth sing to us a joyful, vibrant song.
The fields of green, with flowers newly sprung,
Do greet the eye with colours rich and rare,
And busy bees, with nectar sweetly strung,
Do flit and hum upon the balmy air.
The birds do trill their melodies so sweet,
And chase the shadows with their joyful flight,
And all the world seems perfect and complete,
Bathed in the glory of the golden light.
    So let us live, and taste this sweet delight,
    And hold this beauty, precious, day and night.


The stars above, that twinkle in the night,
Do speak of realms beyond our mortal ken,
And in their vastness, and their silent light,
We glimpse a power far beyond all men.
The moon, that sheds her soft and silver gleam,
Doth cast a magic on the sleeping world,
And in its radiance, we often dream
Of wonders that are yet to be unfurled.
The changing seasons, and the ocean's roar,
The mighty mountains, reaching for the sky,
All speak of forces that we can't ignore,
And bid our humble, mortal spirits fly.
    So let us gaze, and wonder at the sight,
    And find in nature, truth and pure delight.


When winter's frost doth bind the frozen stream,
And chilling winds do whistle through the trees,
My spirit longs for summer's golden dream,
And gentle warmth, and soft, caressing breeze.
The naked branches, reaching for the sky,
Do seem to mourn the verdant leaves now gone,
And in their starkness, I can well descry
The passing of each bright and hopeful dawn.
Yet deep within, a promise doth reside,
That spring will come, and banish winter's reign,
And life renewed, will banish all that died,
And bloom again, with beauty free from pain.
    So let me wait, with patience and with grace,
    For warmer days, and nature's sweet embrace.


Thy constancy, a rock against the tide,
Doth hold me fast when storms begin to brew,
And in thy strength, I safely may abide,
My trusted friend, forever firm and true.
When fickle hearts do waver and do stray,
And promises are broken and forgot,
Thy faithful spirit shines a brighter way,
And anchors me, where others may be not.
The shifting sands, the waters that do churn,
Cannot affect thy purpose, pure and deep,
To thee, my loyalty shall ever turn,
And in thy care, my weary soul shall sleep.
    So let us stand, through all the coming years,
    United firm, dispelling all our fears.


The blush of dawn, that paints the eastern sky,
Doth herald forth a new and hopeful day,
And chases night, and bids the shadows fly,
And chase the lingering darkness far away.
The birds awake, and trill their morning song,
A joyful chorus, rising in the air,
And all the world, where shadows lingered long,
Doth greet the light, and cast away all care.
The dewdrop gleams, upon the grassy blade,
Reflecting heaven, in its tiny sphere,
And nature's beauty, perfectly displayed,
Doth banish doubt, and conquer every fear.
    So let us rise, and greet the sun's warm ray,
    And live with purpose, till the close of day.


Thy love, a treasure, richer than all gold,
Doth fill my heart with an unceasing fire,
A story sweet, that never shall grow old,
The very essence of my soul's desire.
Each tender glance, each softly spoken word,
Doth weave a spell, that holds me in its thrall,
And in thy presence, I am truly stirred,
And gladly yield my spirit, giving all.
The world may rage, and fortunes may depart,
But this true bond, shall never be undone,
A sacred flame, imprinted on my heart,
A love that shines, like never setting sun.
    So let us cherish, this our perfect state,
    And find in love, our everlasting fate.


The fleeting hours, like pilfering thieves, do steal,
The bloom of youth that once adorned my brow,
And leave behind the truths that age reveal,
A wrinkled map of all I was ere now.
My strength doth wane, my vigour starts to fade,
And nimble feet grow slow and prone to rest,
Where once with eager steps I was arrayed,
Now weariness doth hold me in its nest.
Yet in thy love, a fountain fresh I find,
That washes clean the dust of passing years,
And stills the anxious murmurings of my mind,
And dries the well of unavailing tears.
So in thy grace, I find a second spring,
And though I age, thy love makes young my soul sing.


The roseate dawn, with gentle hand, doth paint,
The eastern sky with hues of rose and gold,
Dispelling darkness, making shadows faint,
A lovely story waiting to unfold.
The dewdrop gleams upon the verdant blade,
A tiny jewel, reflecting morning's light,
And nature wakes, in beauty unafraid,
To greet the day and banish shades of night.
So too, my love, when first thy spirit shone,
Upon my soul, a darkness deep and vast,
Thy gentle presence, like a dawn, was known,
A ray of hope, a die forever cast.
And in that light, my heart began to mend,
A love eternal, without earthly end.


The swift-winged hours in hurried sequence fly,
And bear away the moments we hold dear,
As clouds do scud across the summer sky,
And leave behind a void, a silent fear.
The bloom of life, that once did brightly shine,
Now fades and wilts, its former glory past,
And wrinkles tell of seasons that decline,
A legacy of moments that did last.
But in thy gaze, I find a timeless grace,
A beauty that no earthly force can mar,
And in thy love, a safe and dwelling place,
That shines more brightly than the morning star.
So let us seize each moment as it flies,
And build a love that never truly dies.


When stormy winds do rage and skies are grey,
And tempests threaten to our fragile bark,
My spirit quails and longs to fly away,
To find a haven from the chilling dark.
The world's a stage where sorrows often play,
And fortune's frown doth cast a heavy pall,
And hope's faint lamp doth flicker and decay,
As shadows lengthen and as spirits fall.
But then thy voice, a soothing, gentle sound,
Doth whisper courage to my trembling soul,
And in thy strength, a steadfast truth is found,
That makes my shattered spirit once more whole.
So let us face the trials side by side,
With love our shield, and truth our constant guide.


The subtle poison of unkindly words,
Can wound the soul more deeply than a blade,
And break the spirit, like a flock of birds,
That scatter, frightened, from a sudden raid.
A careless jest, a scornful, bitter tone,
Can leave a scar that time can scarce efface,
And sow the seeds of discord, deeply sown,
That choke the tender blooms of loving grace.
But thy sweet counsel, like a gentle balm,
Doth heal the wounds that lesser words impart,
And brings my troubled spirit to a calm,
And stills the tempest raging in my heart.
So let thy kindness be my constant guide,
And let my tongue with gentle truth abide.


The painted mask that worldly men do wear,
Doth hide the truth of their most secret heart,
And hides their malice and their dark despair,
And plays a false and captivating part.
They smile and flatter, with a cunning art,
And promise comfort, though their aim is gain,
And play the player, to a bitter start,
And leave their victims in a sea of pain.
But thy sincere and open, honest gaze,
Doth hold no guile, no artifice, no show,
And in its truth, my spirit finds its ways,
And in thy virtue, I do truly grow.
So let me shun the false, the painted face,
And find my solace in thy honest grace.


The fading embers of a dying fire,
Doth cast a glow, both feeble and so faint,
Reflecting thoughts of lost and vain desire,
And painting shadows, like a weary saint.
The warmth departs, and leaves a chilling air,
Where once the flames did leap with vibrant might,
A silent testament to all despair,
And dimming embers in the coming night.
So too my love, when absent from thy sight,
My spirit chills, and comfort starts to flee,
And all the world is cloaked in darkest night,
Until thy presence brings its warmth to me.
So let us ever be in close embrace,
And keep the fire of love in its true place.


The sturdy oak that braves the winter's blast,
And stands unmoved by winds that fiercely blow,
Doth show a strength that shall forever last,
And mock the frailness of the passing snow.
The ancient rocks that face the ocean's roar,
And bear the brunt of every crashing wave,
Stand firm and constant on the shifting shore,
And from the tide, their very being save.
So is thy love, a bastion strong and deep,
Against the storms of life that rage and swell,
A promise that my heart shall ever keep,
And in its truth, a heaven I can tell.
So let us be like these, in strength aligned,
A love that time and tempest leaves behind.


The fleeting shadow of a passing cloud,
Doth momentarily obscure the sun,
And cast a gloom upon the sunlit crowd,
Before its hurried journey is outrun.
The momentary darkness can deceive,
And make us fear the light has ceased to be,
But in a moment, we can then believe,
The sun still shines, for all the world to see.
So when thy absence casts a shade so deep,
And doubt's dark specter starts to fill my mind,
I know thy love, a promise it doth keep,
And in its light, a brighter truth I find.
So let this gloom but serve to make us prize,
The lasting sunshine of our loving eyes.


The painter's brush, though skilled with subtle hand,
Can ne'er portray the beauty of thy grace,
Nor capture what my eyes can understand,
The perfect form and wonder of thy face.
The poet's quill, though dipped in finest ink,
Can only hint at passions that reside,
And of thy virtues, can but faintly think,
And speak of truths thou dost within thee hide.
For in thy soul, a deeper beauty lies,
Than mortal art can ever hope to claim,
A radiant light that sparkles in thine eyes,
And sets my captive spirit all aflame.
So let my love, though poor, still strive to tell,
The boundless worth within thee, loved so well.


When worldly cares do press with heavy hand,
And steal the peace that makes my spirit whole,
And fill my thoughts with burdens I can't stand,
And take from me my comfort and my soul.
The weight of sorrow doth begin to grow,
And drown the joy that once did brightly gleam,
And all the world seems filled with naught but woe,
And life itself becomes a troubled dream.
But then thy voice, a melody so sweet,
Doth banish shadows, and dispel the fear,
And makes my weary, burdened heart complete,
And brings thy solace ever close and near.
So let me lean on thee, and find my ease,
And in thy love, my troubled spirit please.


The ancient ruins, standing proud and tall,
Doth speak of glories that have long since passed,
Of empires fallen, and of kingdoms' fall,
Whose fleeting grandeur could not ever last.
The stones do whisper tales of by-gone days,
Of heroes' deeds, and battles fought and won,
And cast a spell in their mysterious ways,
Beneath the moon, and in the setting sun.
So shall our love, though time may seek to fray,
And mar the edges of our fondest vows,
Endure beyond the passing of the day,
And bloom anew, like evergreens on boughs.
For in its depth, a strength doth still reside,
That shall against the ravages of time abide.


The fragrant bloom that scents the summer air,
Doth draw the bee with promise of sweet prize,
And in its petals, finds a treasure rare,
Reflected in its many-faceted eyes.
The honeyed dew, that glistens on the leaf,
Doth tempt the ant with sustenance and gain,
And satisfies its hunger, past belief,
And eases hunger, banishes its pain.
So does thy love, a nectar rich and deep,
Doth draw my soul and quench its thirsty fire,
And promises the joy I long to keep,
And fills my heart with ever sweet desire.
So let me feast upon this love so true,
And in its sweetness, find my world anew.


My love, whose eyes like stars do brightly shine,
Doth cast a beam upon my mortal soul,
A heavenly light, a radiance divine,
That makes my shattered spirit strong and whole.
Her voice, a melody that doth enchant,
Doth charm the ear and soothes the troubled breast,
A silken whisper, a melodious chant,
That puts my restless, weary heart to rest.
Her touch, a spark that doth my being fire,
And melts the ice that chilled my lonely days,
A sweet sensation, a fervent desire,
That sets my very inmost soul ablaze.
   So in her presence, all my griefs do cease,
   And I find solace, comfort, and sweet peace.


The creeping time, with unrelenting pace,
Doth steal away the bloom from beauty's cheek,
And carves deep lines upon a youthful face,
And makes the strong and valiant spirit weak.
The raven locks that once did gleam so bright,
Shall turn to silver, thin and scant and grey,
The dancing eyes that shone with youthful light,
Shall dim and fade and vanish quite away.
The sturdy limb that bore a heavy load,
Shall tremble now and scarce support the frame,
The proudest heart that trod a noble road,
Shall yield at last to Time's relentless claim.
   Yet love, if true, shall scorn this mortal blight,
   And burn more fiercely in eternal light.


When I do see the fairest creatures rise,
And feel the stirrings of a warm desire,
I know that Nature, with her subtle lies,
Doth forge the fuel for love's consuming fire.
The blushing rose, the lily pure and white,
The gentle dove that coos with loving sound,
All speak of passion, beauty, and delight,
And in their forms, true love may e'er be found.
But when I turn my gaze to thee, my dear,
My heart doth beat a rhythm swift and bold,
For in thy face, all charms do reappear,
A story of pure beauty to unfold.
   So Nature's bounty seems but pale and cold,
   Compared to thee, whose worth cannot be told.


My muse, thy beauty doth inspire my quill,
To paint in words the wonders of thy grace,
And make the silent, empty pages fill,
With praises due to thy most lovely face.
Thy hair, like threads of spun and golden light,
Doth frame a brow where wisdom hath its seat,
Thy eyes, two pools of deepest, darkest night,
Where truth and gentleness do sweetly meet.
Thy lips, like cherries, ripe and plump and red,
Do promise kisses, soft and warm and deep,
And in their curve, sweet words are softly said,
That lull the weary soul to gentle sleep.
   So let me write, till my last breath is drawn,
   Of thee, my love, from dusk till rosy dawn.


Why doth the sun, that shines with golden ray,
Not warm my heart when thou art far from me?
The brightest noon doth seem a dreary day,
When from my sight thy presence doth decree.
The birds do sing, their carols sweet and clear,
But their glad songs do mock my lonely state,
No joy can pierce the veil of doubt and fear,
When absence seals my melancholy fate.
The gentle breeze that stirs the verdant leaves,
Doth whisper tales of solace and of rest,
But to my soul, no comfort it receives,
Whilst thou, my love, art absent from my breast.
   So let me pine, and in my sorrow drown,
   Until thy smile shall wear love's radiant crown.


The world doth oft with empty words deceive,
And praise the deed that hath no honest core,
The hollow flatterer, we do believe,
And cast aside the truth we should adore.
So many speak with tongues of honeyed art,
And weave their webs of falsehood and of lies,
To win the favour of a foolish heart,
And blind the unwatchful, unsuspecting eyes.
But thy plain speech, though blunt it may appear,
Doth hold a truth that shines with steady flame,
More precious far than promises held dear,
That fade and vanish, leaving naught but shame.
   So let me hold thy honest words in store,
   And love their truth, and ask for nothing more.


When I reflect upon the summer's end,
And see the leaves of gold and crimson fall,
I know that all things beautiful must bend,
And yield at last to Nature's solemn call.
The fervent heat that warmed the sun-drenched days,
Doth fade and dwindle, leaving naught but chill,
The vibrant hues that filled the verdant maze,
Do pale and wither, standing stark and still.
So too, my youth, that once did brightly burn,
Must dim and fade, and leave me weak and old,
To all of Nature's changes I must turn,
And in her ancient lessons be controlled.
   Yet love, like spring, can bid the earth revive,
   And keep my heart eternally alive.


When outward shows do blind the common eye,
And men do judge by garb and outward mien,
They fail to see the spirit soaring high,
Or know the virtue that lies deep unseen.
The shining armour, and the noble crest,
May hide a craven heart, a soul of dread,
Whilst humble raiment may conceal the best,
And noblest thoughts within a lowly head.
So let me look beyond the outward guise,
And seek the essence of the soul within,
Where truth and goodness ever shall arise,
And where true beauty truly doth begin.
   For in thy heart, I find a noble grace,
   That shines more brightly than a kingly face.


When winter's chill doth grip the barren land,
And icy winds do bite with frosty breath,
The world lies stark, beneath a frozen hand,
And seems to slumber in a gentle death.
The trees stand bare, their leafy crowns all gone,
The rivers cease their murmuring, soft song,
And darkness reigns from dusk till early dawn,
Where life and warmth did lately dance along.
But in my heart, a fire doth brightly burn,
A steadfast flame that Time cannot subdue,
And to its warmth, my spirit shall return,
And find its strength and comfort there anew.
   For love like thine, a constant, guiding star,
   Doth warm me best, no matter where we are.


My quill doth falter, and my ink runs dry,
When I attempt to speak of thy great worth,
For human tongue can never truly vie,
With all the wonders that thou dost give birth.
The painter's brush, the sculptor's patient hand,
May strive to capture beauty, fair and bright,
But none can fully on this mortal land,
Embody all thy pure and radiant light.
Thy grace, a mystery beyond compare,
Thy virtue, a star that doth forever gleam,
Thy kindness, a balm to banish all despair,
A waking truth within a blissful dream.
   So let my verse, though humble it may be,
   Proclaim the endless excellence of thee.


When envious tongues do wag with spiteful sound,
And whisper tales of slander and of blame,
They seek to drag my honour to the ground,
And cast a shadow on my honest name.
But falsehood's darts, though sharp they may appear,
Can never pierce the armour of my truth,
Nor can their venom cause a single tear,
When guided by the strength of constant youth.
For in thy heart, a judgment pure and right,
Doth see beyond the malice and the lies,
And in that faith, I find my greatest might,
And smile at all their petty, vain surprise.
   So let them speak, their malice shall not win,
   When thy true love doth ever dwell within.


When in the quiet of the evening's hush,
My thoughts do turn to thee, my dearest love,
My heart doth beat with an ecstatic rush,
As if to soar with angels high above.
The moon doth cast its soft and silvery gleam,
Upon the world, now slumbering and still,
And in its light, I see a waking dream,
That doth my very soul with rapture fill.
Thy image, fair, doth haunt my waking hours,
And in my sleep, thy presence I embrace,
More sweet than nectar from the fairest flowers,
More comforting than any earthly place.
   So let me dwell within this loving trance,
   And in thy memory, forever dance.


When Phoebus doth his golden chariot guide,
And chases hence the shadows of the night,
My soul awakes, with hopeful spirit eyed,
And longs to bask within his radiant light.
But ah, that light, though fair it may appear,
Doth blind the eye that gazes on its gleam,
And fills my heart with a most anxious fear,
That all is but a fleeting, painted dream.
For though the day may promise joy and mirth,
And banish all the sorrows of the dark,
It cannot soothe the aching of the earth,
Nor quench the flames that in my spirit spark.
   So is my love, a sun that shines too bright,
   And drowns my senses in its endless light.


The rose, that blushes with the morning dew,
And scents the air with perfume sweet and rare,
Though fair it blooms, its beauty is but through
A passing hour, that vanishes in air.
So is thy grace, a blossom on the vine,
That doth enthrall my senses with its hue,
But ere I clasp it, doth thy favour pine,
And leave me longing for what was so true.
The summer's lease hath all too short a date,
And winter's chill doth hasten its decay,
So must thy beauty yield to cruel fate,
And from my sight be borne ere close of day.
   Yet in my heart, thy memory shall reside,
   A phantom bloom, where joys and sorrows hide.


Thy voice, a music that doth stir my soul,
And fills the void where silent sorrows dwelled,
Doth make my wounded spirit once more whole,
And all my worldly cares are gently quelled.
When thou dost speak, the nightingale doth cease,
To shame its song with melodies so rare,
And gentle zephyrs whisper words of peace,
That banish every trouble and despair.
Oh, would that I could hoard each precious sound,
And keep it safe within my loving breast,
That when the storm of silence doth surround,
My heart might find its comfort and its rest.
   But alas, the ear can hold but fleeting sound,
   And in its absence, sorrow doth abound.


The fleeting hours, that swift do pass us by,
And bear away our youth with hurried pace,
Do make me fear to see them fade and die,
And leave behind no vestige of their grace.
For beauty's bloom, though vibrant it may seem,
Is but a transient guest upon the bough,
And life itself, a momentary gleam,
That vanishes ere we can grasp it now.
So let us seize the moments as they fly,
And hold them dear, ere they be lost to time,
For in their passing, we ourselves shall die,
And leave no echo of our earthly prime.
   Yet in thy love, a timeless beauty dwells,
   That conquers death, and all its mortal spells.


When absence doth thy dear form hide from view,
And lonely hours do drag with leaden feet,
My soul doth yearn for tidings sent from you,
And longs to hear thy voice, so pure and sweet.
The sun may shine, the birds may sing their song,
But all is barren, empty, and forlorn,
Without thy presence, where I do belong,
My heart doth ache, and weary till the morn.
I count the minutes, and I mark the days,
Until again thy radiant face I see,
And chase away these melancholy ways,
That absence hath inflicted unto me.
   So haste thee back, my love, my guiding star,
   And heal the wound that keeps us now so far.


The bloom of youth, though fair it doth appear,
Is but a season, destined soon to fade,
And beauty's hour, though held so passing dear,
Is but a shadow, by the daylight made.
For time, the thief, doth steal with silent tread,
And wrinkles carve where smoothness once did lie,
And strength departs, and vigour lies instead,
Beneath the gaze of his unyielding eye.
So let us not in fleeting beauty trust,
Nor deem our prime eternal and our own,
But build our love on worth that is not dust,
A stronger foundation, where it can be known.
   For outward grace will wither and decay,
   But inner truth shall last beyond its day.


When I behold the stars in heaven's vault,
And see the moon in silver splendour gleam,
My soul doth yearn, and finds itself at fault,
To think that such perfection is a dream.
The cosmic dance, so ordered and so grand,
Doth stir within me wonder and delight,
And makes me feel a part of that vast land,
That stretches far beyond my mortal sight.
And in that vision, I do see thy face,
Reflected there, a star among the night,
Whose gentle beams do fill this lonely space,
And fill my heart with everlasting light.
   So in the heavens, and in thee I find,
   A beauty that doth captivate my mind.


The winter's chill doth bite with icy breath,
And strips the trees of all their leafy pride,
And nature seems to welcome silent death,
Where all the summer's verdure doth now hide.
The world is clad in garments of the snow,
And silence reigns where birds did sweetly sing,
And all is barren, where the streams do flow,
And life's bright colours to the frost do cling.
But in my heart, a summer doth remain,
A constant warmth, that winter cannot chill,
For in thy love, I find a sweet refrain,
That makes my weary spirit once more thrill.
   So let the world lie frozen and asleep,
   My love for thee, its warmth shall ever keep.


My verse, though humble, doth attempt to trace,
The noble outline of thy virtues rare,
And in each line, I seek to find a place,
To show the world how much thy soul doth bear.
But oh, my skill doth lag behind my will,
And words do falter, when I would impart,
The depth of feeling that doth my heart fill,
And burns with ardour, like a lover's dart.
So let my efforts be a willing plea,
To show the love that in my spirit lies,
And though imperfect, may it speak for me,
And bring a smile unto thy loving eyes.
   For though my ink be weak, and paper thin,
   My heart's true tribute lies deep within.


When winter's chill doth grip the barren land,
And nature sleeps beneath a snowy shroud,
My thoughts, like birds, do fly at thy command,
And seek thy warmth amidst the frozen crowd.
The icicles, like tears, do hang so low,
From eaves where summer's joy did once reside,
And biting winds do fiercely keen and blow,
Where laughter echoed and where love did guide.
But in my heart, a spring doth ever bloom,
A verdant paradise, untouched by frost,
Dispelling all the winter's chilling gloom,
And counting not the treasures I have lost.
    For thou art spring, and summer, and the sun,
    My dearest love, till all my days are done.


The raven's wing doth cast a shadow deep,
Upon the hopes that danced with morning's gleam,
And sorrow's torrents from my eyes do leap,
Lost in the phantoms of a waking dream.
The world, once bright with colours rich and bold,
Now wears a garb of dull and sombre grey,
And tales of joy are but as legends old,
That whisper of a long-departed day.
Yet in this darkness, still thy face I see,
A guiding light, a solace to my pain,
And though despair doth seek to conquer me,
Thy memory doth make me strong again.
    So let me cling to thee, my guiding star,
    And heal my wounds, no matter where they are.


The silent night, with watchful, starry eye,
Doth witness to the secrets I do keep,
Of passion's fire that will not ever die,
And dreams of thee, when weary senses sleep.
The moonbeams paint a path upon the ground,
That leads my yearning spirit to thy door,
Where solace in thy presence may be found,
And all my earthly cares shall be no more.
Though shadows lengthen and the hours grow late,
My faithful heart doth beat for thee alone,
And waits to share with thee a common fate,
Upon love's sweet and undisputed throne.
    So let us join our hands and hearts as one,
    And bid farewell to all that is undone.


The tempest's rage, the thunder's fearsome sound,
May shake the earth and rend the sky apart,
But in thy love, a refuge I have found,
A steadfast anchor to my beating heart.
Let lightning flash and winds begin to roar,
And waves of sorrow crash upon the shore,
I shall not falter, nor my will ignore,
For thou art strength, and shall my heart restore.
Though trials come, and fortune prove unkind,
And darkling clouds obscure the hopeful day,
Thy constancy, a solace to my mind,
Shall guide me safely on my onward way.
    So let the world its fiercest fury vent,
    My love for thee is not for years but meant.


Thy beauty shines, a beacon in the night,
More radiant than the stars in heaven's dome,
It fills my breast with wonder and delight,
And guides my spirit to its rightful home.
Thy voice, a music sweeter than the lute,
Doth soothe my soul and banish all my fears,
A melody that never can grow mute,
That whispers solace to my falling tears.
Thy smile, a dawn that chases gloom away,
Doth paint the world with colours rich and bright,
And bids my frozen heart begin to play,
A joyful tune, bathed in celestial light.
So let me gaze upon thee, day by day,
And lose myself within thy wondrous ray.


The fleeting hours, like pilfering thieves, do steal
The bloom of youth, that once did grace my cheek,
And leave behind the ravages they deal,
A testament to time, both strong and weak.
The silver threads now mingle with the gold,
Upon my brow, where once youth's banner flew,
And stories of the passing years are told,
In lines etched deep, forever fresh and new.
Yet in thine eyes, a spark of youth remains,
A timeless flame that mocks the hastening years,
And through the winter's bleak and chilling rains,
Thy spirit laughs, dispelling all my fears.
So let us cherish moments, ere they cease,
And find in love our everlasting peace.


My love for thee, a boundless, surging sea,
Doth know no ebb, no limit, and no end,
It flows and ebbs eternally for thee,
A constant tide, on which my heart doth send
Its deepest vows, its faith, its every sigh,
To anchor fast within thy loving breast,
And ne'er to waver, ne'er to fade or die,
But in thy favour find its final rest.
The storms of life may rage and winds may blow,
And threaten to submerge this fragile bark,
But still my love, like coral, will grow,
And light the ocean with its burning spark.
So fear thou not, my dearest, hold me near,
My steadfast love shall banish every fear.


The roseate blush that paints thy tender cheek,
Doth shame the dawn, and make the sun retire,
Its fragile beauty, which all hearts do seek,
Sets all my dormant passions into fire.
Thy silken hair, a cascade of the night,
Doth frame a face where all perfection lies,
And in thine eyes, I see a radiant light,
That mirrors heaven in their deep surprise.
When thou dost speak, a symphony I hear,
More sweet than any Nightingale's soft call,
And all my doubts and troubles disappear,
Beneath the charm that doth my senses thrall.
So let me live within this vision fair,
And breathe the perfumed sweetness of thy hair.


The fickle crowd doth praise and then condemn,
With changing winds their fickle favour turns,
They pluck the flower, then they crush the stem,
And quench the fire that brightly in them burns.
Today they crown, tomorrow they revile,
Their shifting moods no constancy can know,
And he who trusts their favour for a while,
Shall find himself cast down from high to low.
But in thy heart, a truth doth ever dwell,
A steadfast loyalty, a gentle grace,
That shields me from the scorn they loudly yell,
And finds for me a safe and sheltered place.
So let them mock and carp with envious eye,
Thy faithful love shall lift me to the sky.


The world's applause, a momentary gleam,
That fades as swiftly as a morning mist,
A hollow echo of a fleeting dream,
By fickle fortune on the wind is kissed.
The cheers that greet a victor on his day,
Soon turn to jeers when fortune's wheel doth turn,
And those who basked in glory's golden ray,
Must learn to bear the scorn and keenly burn.
But thy regard, a jewel of purest worth,
Doth shine more brightly than the starry host,
It is the anchor of my soul on earth,
The truest treasure that I value most.
So let the masses clamour and pursue,
My dearest prize, my guiding star, is you.


The winter's frost doth bind the fertile ground,
And silence reigns where summer's birds did sing,
No verdant blade nor blooming flower is found,
And chilling winds their mournful dirge do bring.
The trees stand stark, their branches bare and cold,
As if in grief for summers passed away,
And Nature's tales of beauty, once so bold,
Are locked in slumber, hidden from the day.
Yet in this chill, my love for thee doth bloom,
A constant warmth that melts the icy spell,
And banishes the encroaching winter's gloom,
Within the haven where thy virtues dwell.
So let the season rage with all its might,
My heart shall glow with love's eternal light.


Thy gentle touch, a balm to soothe my soul,
Doth chase away the shadows of despair,
And makes my wounded spirit once more whole,
And lifts the burden that I sadly bear.
Thy whispered words, like music on the breeze,
Do calm the tempest raging in my breast,
And bring my troubled, anxious heart to ease,
And grant my weary spirit blessed rest.
O, would that I could hold thee ever near,
And feel the comfort of thy sweet embrace,
To banish every doubt and every fear,
And find within thine eyes my resting place.
So let us pledge our troth, and ever be,
My dearest love, eternally with thee.


When fortune smiles, and skies are clear and bright,
And all the world doth seem a joyful stage,
Then friendships sprout and bloom in pure delight,
And flatterers do fill their flattering page.
They praise thy deeds, they laud thy noble name,
And feast with thee, with laughter and with wine,
But when the clouds obscure the sun's warm flame,
They melt away, like shadows in a shrine.
But thou, my friend, art constant as the star,
That guides the sailor through the darkest night,
Thy faithful heart doth shine from near and far,
And lends my weary spirit strength and light.
So let the fickle crowd their favours lend,
Thy constant truth shall be my dearest friend.


The rose doth fade, its petals fall to ground,
The lily droops, its perfume starts to cease,
The summer's bloom no longer can be found,
And all the earth doth languish in decrease.
The golden light that gilded summer's day,
Now yields to autumn's melancholic hue,
And all the joys that made the heart to play,
Are lost and gone, like morning's early dew.
But still thy love, a springtime ever new,
Doth burst with life, and banish winter's chill,
A constant bloom, forever fresh and true,
That bids my lonely, waiting heart be still.
So let the seasons change and pass away,
Thy love shall be my bright and summer's day.


I swear by all the heavens high above,
And by the earth beneath my weary feet,
My constancy, my loyalty, my love,
Shall ne'er grow cold, nor ever know defeat.
Though tempests roar and oceans surge and swell,
And rocks and shoals my fragile bark assail,
My steadfast heart shall break their angry spell,
And through the fiercest gale, shall never fail.
For in thy eyes, a truth I can behold,
A promise whispered, faithful and sincere,
A treasure richer than the purest gold,
That banishes all doubt and every fear.
So let us build our trust, and build it strong,
And love shall be our everlasting song.


The envious eye doth seek to dim thy grace,
And whisper tales that slander and defame,
They strive to tarnish thy most lovely face,
And cast upon thy virtue burning shame.
But calumny, like lightning, swift doth fly,
And leaves no mark where purity doth dwell,
It cannot wound, though aiming for the sky,
